macro_segments.title: Overview of Nissan as Rosh Hashanah and the Bal Tacher Framework

micro_segments.title: Introduction to today's daf and the concept of Nissan as Rosh Hashanah l'regalim
Discussion of the obligation to remain overnight in Jerusalem and the timing of sacrifices
Connection of Pesach and Sukkot verses to establish the overnight requirement and seasonal differences

macro_segments.title: Tashlumin (Compensation) on Shavuot and the Nature of Holiday Obligations

micro_segments.title: The positions of Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Shimon regarding three regalim and tashlumin on Shavuot
Rabba bar Shmuel's teaching on counting days and weeks as basis for Shavuot's dual nature
The conceptual framework of Shavuot as both a one-day Yom Tov and a week-long holiday for sacrifices

macro_segments.title: The List of Obligations Subject to Bal Tacher and Its Logical Problems

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Baraita's list of items subject to bal tacher (damim, erchin, charamim, hekdeshot, chataot, ashamot, olot, shelamim, tzedakot, maasrot, bechor)
Examination of Pesach's inclusion in the list and Rav Chisda's explanation that it serves no purpose
Rav Sheshes's interpretation of Pesach as referring to shelamim that converted from a Pesach sacrifice
Distinction between shelamim from Pesach and regular shelamim, and the implications for bal tacher
Overview of the conceptual problems with the bal tacher list: damim and erchin, hekdeshot, chataot and ashamot, and the role of vows

macro_segments.title: Derivation of the Bal Tacher Obligations from the Parashat Nedenim

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's derivation of vows and free-will offerings (nedavah) from the verse 'Ki tidor neder'
Extension of nedavah to include sanctified animals directly (harei zo olah)
Inclusion of damim, erchin, charamim, and hekdeshot as things vowed to God
Use of 'Ki darosh yidrashenu' to extend bal tacher to obligations not based on vows (chataot, ashamot, olot, shelamim)
Use of 'Hashem Elokecha' to derive tzedakah, maasrot, and bechor as falling under bal tacher

macro_segments.title: The List of Obligations Subject to Bal Tacher and Its Logical Problems (continued)

micro_segments.title: Analysis of tzedakah, maasrot, and the surprising inclusion of leket, shikcha, and peah as obligations subject to bal tacher

macro_segments.title: Derivation of the Bal Tacher Obligations from the Parashat Nedenim (continued)

micro_segments.title: Use of 'me'imach' to derive leket, shikcha, and peah as obligations to those in one's community

macro_segments.title: The Principle That Bal Tacher Invalidates the Owner But Not the Offering (Lo Tacher Leshalmo)

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's statement that the transgression applies only to the offering itself, not its substitute
Definition of chilufim (substitutes): animals sanctified as replacements for lost or defective offerings
Case of olah and shelamim substitutes that can be brought and incur bal tacher obligation
Case of chatat substitutes that are not brought (animal must die) and thus do not incur bal tacher
The Gemara's challenge: why does the verse need to exclude substitutes if there is no bal tacher anyway?
Rav Sheshes's answer: the case involves a todah that mixed with its substitute, illustrating a scenario where the substitute status matters

macro_segments.title: Chilufim (Substitutes) in the Context of Defective Offerings and Transfer of Sanctity

micro_segments.title: Scenario where an offering develops a mum (blemish) and the sanctity is transferred to another animal
Rava's explanation for Rabbi Meir: when a mum occurs mid-holiday and sanctity is transferred, one counts the regalim from the new animal
The Gemara's derivation that when sanctity transfers to a new animal, the clock for the three regalim restarts
Comparison between neder (vow on oneself) and nedavah (direct sanctification) in determining whose obligation it is

macro_segments.title: The Principle That Bal Tacher Sins in the Owner But Not in the Offering (Ve-hayah Becha Cheit)

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's statement that the sin falls on the owner but the offering remains valid
Parallel case in Rabbi Meir's teaching on bechor: using the connection to maaser to show offerings are not invalidated by late bringing
The Gemara's question: can we not derive this validity from the bechor/maaser connection alone?
Distinction between bechor (a gift, not dependent on ratzon/divine favor) and other offerings that are dependent on ratzon
Conceptual difference between bechor and maaser (sanctification acts) versus olah and shelamim (connection to God)
Ben Azzai's teaching on pigul: why the verse lo tacher leshalmo is necessary to show bal tacher offerings are still ratzon despite the transgression

topical_tags.term: Bal tacher (not delaying vows and obligations)
Tashlumin (making up sacrifices within extended period)
Shavuot as both day and week
Damim, erchin, charamim (monetary vows and valuations)
Maasrot (tithes) and maaser rishon, maaser sheni, maaser ani
Leket, shikcha, peah (agricultural gifts to poor)
Pigul (invalid sacrifice due to improper intention)
Mitzvah haba'ah b'aveirah (commandment performed through transgression)
Kasher (valid) offerings despite bal tacher violation
Psukei pasukim (connecting verses to derive laws)
